questions.question: What protocol does a host use to learn the MAC address associated with an IP address?
questions.explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) maps IP addresses to MAC addresses. The request is broadcast; the reply is unicast.

questions.question: What is the destination MAC address of an ARP request?
questions.explanation: ARP requests use the broadcast MAC FFFF.FFFF.FFFF so every host on the segment receives it. The reply is unicast because the sender's IP and MAC were already learned from the request.

questions.question: As a packet traverses multiple routers, what happens to the IP addresses and MAC addresses?
options: Both IP and MAC change at every hop
IP changes per hop, MAC stays the same
Source/destination IPs stay the same; source/destination MACs change at every hop
questions.explanation: The original source and destination IPs are preserved end-to-end. At each router hop, the packet is de-encapsulated and re-encapsulated with new source/destination MAC addresses.

questions.question: What does a router do with a packet that has no matching route in its routing table?
options: Sends it to the default gateway
Buffers it until a route appears
questions.explanation: Routers drop packets that have no matching route (including no default route). Switches flood unknown unicast frames — routers do not.

questions.question: What is encapsulation at Layer 2 in the forwarding process?
options: Adding an IP header to the segment
Wrapping the packet in an Ethernet frame with MAC header and trailer
questions.explanation: Layer 2 encapsulation wraps the packet in an Ethernet frame (MAC header + trailer) before transmission. The receiver de-encapsulates to examine the Layer 3 packet.

questions.question: What does BIA stand for in the context of a router interface?
questions.explanation: BIA (Burned-In Address) is the hardware MAC assigned by the manufacturer. You can override it with `mac-address` on the interface, but the BIA remains.

questions.question: A Windows PC user wants to see the IP, subnet mask, default gateway, and MAC. Which command?
questions.explanation: `ipconfig /all` on Windows displays IP, subnet mask, default gateway, and physical (MAC) address. `ifconfig` is Linux/macOS.

questions.question: What is a default gateway?
options: The DNS server an end host queries
The router end hosts send packets to when the destination is outside the local subnet
The switch that connects all VLANs
The DHCP server for the subnet
questions.explanation: The default gateway is the router that end hosts send packets to when the destination is outside their local subnet. It's an older term for a router.

questions.question: What protocol does `ping` use to test reachability?
questions.explanation: Ping uses ICMP echo request and echo reply messages to test reachability between two hosts at Layer 3.

questions.question: Why is an ARP reply unicast instead of broadcast?
options: Because the responder already learned the requester's IP and MAC from the broadcast ARP request
Because ARP replies must be encrypted
Because broadcasts are blocked by default
questions.explanation: The requester's IP and MAC are included in the ARP request, so the responder has everything needed to unicast the reply directly back.

questions.question: What is the difference between encapsulation and re-encapsulation on a routed path?
options: Encapsulation happens once at the source; re-encapsulation happens at each router hop with new MAC headers
Encapsulation is for IPv4 only, re-encapsulation is for IPv6
Re-encapsulation adds an extra Layer 3 header
questions.explanation: The source encapsulates once. Each router then de-encapsulates, checks Layer 3, and re-encapsulates in a new Ethernet frame with updated MAC addresses for the next hop.

questions.question: Which command manually sets the MAC address on a router interface?
questions.explanation: `mac-address aaaa.aaaa.aaaa` under the interface overrides the BIA with a manually configured MAC.
